state_description: Waiting for human approval of the plan
product_description: Semantic search engine with vector embeddings, hybrid search (BM25 + vector), query understanding, auto-complete, faceted filtering, and relevance tuning console.

plan.description: Define product requirements, success metrics, and scope for the semantic search engine including vector embeddings, hybrid search, query understanding, auto-complete, faceted filtering, and relevance tuning console.
acceptance_criteria: PRD document lists all features with acceptance criteria
Success metrics defined (latency p99 < 200ms, recall@10 > 0.85)
Embedding model candidates shortlisted with trade-off matrix
Vector DB options evaluated (Qdrant vs Weaviate vs Pinecone)

plan.description: Design the data model and document ingestion pipeline: chunking strategy, metadata schema, embedding generation pipeline, BM25 index construction, and incremental update support.
acceptance_criteria: Document schema defined with fields: id, content, chunks, metadata, embeddings, facet_fields
Chunking pipeline handles PDF, HTML, plain text inputs
Embedding pipeline generates 384-dim vectors for each chunk
BM25 inverted index built and serializable to disk
Incremental index update adds documents without full rebuild
Pipeline processes 1000 docs/min on standard hardware

plan.description: Set up the embedding model pipeline: select and fine-tune sentence transformer, build query encoder, implement query understanding module (intent classification, query expansion, spell correction), and design relevance scoring.
acceptance_criteria: Embedding model loads and encodes query in < 50ms
Query expansion generates 3-5 semantically related terms
Spell correction handles common typos with > 90% accuracy
Intent classifier distinguishes navigational/informational/transactional queries
Hybrid RRF score combines BM25 + vector scores with configurable alpha weight
Model evaluation script outputs NDCG@10 and MRR metrics
Bias evaluation performed
Model card generated

plan.description: Provision and configure the vector database (Qdrant), BM25 index store, metadata/facets store (PostgreSQL), and auto-complete trie/prefix index.
acceptance_criteria: Qdrant collection created with correct vector dimensions and HNSW params
PostgreSQL schema includes documents, facets, and query_logs tables with migrations
Redis sorted set populated for prefix-based auto-complete lookups
GIN index on facets JSONB column enables sub-10ms facet aggregation
All DB schemas version-controlled as migration files

plan.description: Build the core search backend service: hybrid search orchestrator (BM25 + vector fusion), query processing pipeline, faceted filtering engine, and indexing API.
acceptance_criteria: POST /index accepts documents, chunks, embeds, and stores in Qdrant + BM25
POST /search returns hybrid-ranked results with scores and metadata
Facet filters applied as pre-filter to vector search and BM25 simultaneously
RRF fusion configurable via alpha weight parameter
Search latency p95 < 150ms for corpus of 100k documents
Indexing throughput > 500 docs/min end-to-end

plan.description: Implement auto-complete service with prefix matching, semantic suggestion ranking, and personalization hooks. Implement query understanding middleware that runs spell correction and query expansion before search.
acceptance_criteria: GET /autocomplete?q=prefix returns top-10 suggestions in < 20ms
Suggestions ranked by query frequency and semantic similarity to partial query
POST /query/understand returns corrected query, expanded terms, and detected intent
Query pipeline adds < 30ms overhead to total search latency
Auto-complete index updated incrementally as new queries arrive

plan.description: Build the relevance tuning engine: configurable boosting rules, field weights, A/B testing framework for ranking experiments, and query-level override storage.
acceptance_criteria: Boost rules support field-level, recency, and popularity signals
A/B experiment router splits traffic by configurable percentage
Query overrides (pin, exclude, boost specific doc) stored and applied at query time
Relevance config changes hot-reload without service restart
Click-through and dwell-time feedback events logged for offline evaluation

plan.description: Design and document the full OpenAPI spec for all search_engine endpoints: /index, /search, /autocomplete, /query/understand, /facets, /relevance/config, /relevance/experiment, /analytics.
acceptance_criteria: OpenAPI 3.1 spec covers all endpoints with request/response schemas
All query parameters, filters, and pagination fields documented
Error response codes and formats specified for each endpoint
Spec validates with openapi-validator with zero errors
Example requests/responses included for every endpoint

plan.description: Design the search UI (search bar with auto-complete, results page with facets) and the relevance tuning console (boost rules editor, experiment dashboard, query analytics).
acceptance_criteria: Wireframes for all 5 pages reviewed and approved
Auto-complete dropdown shows suggestions with keyboard navigation
Facet sidebar supports multi-select, range filters, and result counts
Boost rule editor supports drag-and-drop field weight sliders
Experiment dashboard shows side-by-side NDCG comparison for A/B variants
WCAG 2.1 AA accessibility checklist passed for all components

plan.description: Build the search frontend: search bar with live auto-complete, results page with highlighted snippets, faceted filter sidebar, pagination, and result card components.
acceptance_criteria: SearchBar fires autocomplete requests with 150ms debounce
Auto-complete dropdown renders top-10 suggestions with keyboard nav (arrow keys + enter)
Results page displays highlighted matched terms in snippets
Facet sidebar updates result count on each filter selection without full page reload
Pagination supports cursor-based navigation
Search results render in < 300ms from API response

plan.description: Build the relevance tuning console: boost rules editor, field weight sliders, query override manager, A/B experiment wizard, and real-time analytics dashboard.
acceptance_criteria: Boost rules CRUD UI calls PUT /relevance/config and reflects changes immediately
Field weight sliders show live preview of ranking change on sample query
Query override table supports pin/exclude/boost actions per document
A/B wizard creates experiment with traffic split, name, and variant config
Analytics dashboard shows top queries, zero-result queries, and CTR over time
All console actions require confirmation dialog before applying to production

plan.description: Containerize all services with Docker Compose for local development and Kubernetes manifests for production: search API, Qdrant, PostgreSQL, Redis, and frontend. Set up CI/CD pipeline.
acceptance_criteria: docker compose up --build starts full stack with zero manual steps
All services pass health checks within 60s of startup
Kubernetes manifests include resource limits, liveness/readiness probes
CI pipeline runs on every PR: lint → test → build in < 10 min
Staging deployment automatically triggered on merge to main
Secrets managed via Kubernetes secrets, not hardcoded in manifests

plan.description: Write unit tests for all backend modules: hybrid search orchestrator, BM25 retriever, vector retriever, RRF fusion, facet filter engine, query understanding pipeline, and relevance tuning engine.
acceptance_criteria: Unit test coverage >= 85% across all backend modules
RRF fusion tested with known score inputs and expected rank outputs
Facet filter tests verify correct AND/OR filter logic across multiple facets
Query understanding tests cover spell correction, expansion, and intent cases
All tests run in < 60s with mocked external dependencies
CI pipeline fails on any test regression

plan.description: Write integration and end-to-end tests: full index-then-search flow, hybrid ranking correctness, auto-complete accuracy, facet filter accuracy, and relevance config hot-reload.
acceptance_criteria: Index 1000-doc corpus and retrieve top-10 for 50 benchmark queries
Hybrid search NDCG@10 >= BM25-only NDCG@10 on test corpus
Facet filter integration test confirms filtered results all match filter criteria
Auto-complete test verifies prefix 'sea' returns results containing 'search' in top-5
Relevance config change reflected in search ranking within 1 request after hot-reload
Playwright E2E test covers: type query → see autocomplete → select → see results → apply facet

plan.description: Build offline evaluation harness: NDCG, MRR, Recall@K metrics computation, relevance judgment dataset creation tooling, and regression benchmarks for ranking quality.
acceptance_criteria: Evaluation script accepts run file (query→ranked_docs) and qrel file, outputs all metrics
Baseline BM25-only, vector-only, and hybrid runs all benchmarked and compared
Regression test fails CI if NDCG@10 drops > 2% from baseline
Per-query breakdown identifies top-20 hardest queries for debugging
Evaluation report auto-generated as HTML artifact on each CI run
Bias evaluation performed
Model card generated

plan.description: Security review: input sanitization for search queries and index payloads, rate limiting, authentication for tuning console, and threat model for the search API.
acceptance_criteria: Search query inputs sanitized against injection patterns before processing
Rate limiting enforced: 100 req/min per IP for search, 10 req/min for index
Relevance tuning console requires JWT authentication
Payload size limit of 10MB enforced on /index endpoint
Threat model document covers vector poisoning, data exfiltration, and DoS vectors
OWASP Top 10 checklist completed with findings addressed

plan.description: Set up monitoring and observability: search latency histograms, index throughput metrics, zero-result query rate, Qdrant health metrics, and alerting rules.
acceptance_criteria: Grafana dashboard shows all key metrics with 15s refresh
Prometheus scrapes search API metrics endpoint
Alert fires within 2 minutes of p99 latency exceeding 500ms
Zero-result query rate alert enables proactive tuning interventions
Runbook linked from each alert with investigation steps
Dashboard exported as JSON and committed to repo

plan.description: Write technical documentation: architecture overview, API reference, indexing guide, relevance tuning guide, and operator runbook.
acceptance_criteria: Architecture doc includes system diagram showing all components and data flows
API reference auto-generated from OpenAPI spec with examples
Indexing guide covers document schema, chunking config, and incremental updates
Relevance tuning guide explains boost rules, field weights, and A/B experiments with examples
Operator runbook covers scaling Qdrant, reindexing, and backup/restore procedures
Quickstart gets a developer to first search result in < 15 minutes

flaws: Step 8 (OpenAPI spec) depends on steps 5–7 (implementation) — this is design-after-code, not design-first. API contracts should be agreed before implementation to prevent schema drift and undocumented edge cases. The entire dependency chain is inverted here.

Step 6 autocomplete uses 'redis_prefix_scan + semantic_rerank' but targets < 20ms. Redis SCAN is O(N) and semantic reranking adds 30–80ms per call. These two constraints are physically incompatible unless reranking is fully pre-computed and cached — which is not specified.

Step 5 states facet filters are 'applied as pre-filter to vector search and BM25 simultaneously.' Qdrant pre-filtering degrades HNSW recall severely when selectivity is high (< 5% of corpus matches filter). Post-filtering or filtered HNSW (payload-indexed) trade-offs are not addressed — this will silently return poor results for selective filters.

Step 7 A/B experiment router has no statistical rigor: no minimum sample size, no significance threshold (p-value or Bayesian), no mechanism to determine when an experiment is conclusive. Traffic split percentage alone does not constitute a valid A/B framework. Shipping ranking changes based on inconclusive experiments is worse than no experiments.

Index consistency between Qdrant (vector) and BM25 (disk-serialized inverted index) is never addressed. A partial write failure leaves them diverged with no detection mechanism, no compensating transaction, and no reconciliation process. For hybrid search this is a correctness failure, not a latency issue.

Step 3 intent classifier (navigational/informational/transactional) has no training data source specified, no labeling process, no fallback behavior when confidence is low, and no acceptance criterion for the training set size. This is described as a deliverable but has no implementation path.

Step 2 claims '1000 docs/min on standard hardware' without specifying hardware. all-MiniLM-L6-v2 encoding on CPU achieves approximately 100–300 docs/min per core depending on chunk size. This target is probably unachievable without GPU or multi-worker parallelism, neither of which is planned.

Step 9 (UX design) only depends on step 1, but the relevance tuning console design should also depend on step 7. Technical constraints discovered in step 7 (e.g., hot-reload mechanics, boost rule schema) will force redesign of the console UI, discovered after wireframes are already approved.

Cursor-based pagination (step 10) is incompatible with dynamic relevance configs. If a relevance config changes between pages, cursor positions become invalid — documents can be duplicated or skipped across pages. No invalidation strategy is defined.

Step 15 regression test 'fails CI if NDCG@10 drops > 2%' requires a stable stored baseline. No mechanism for versioning, storing, or updating the baseline is specified. If the baseline is recomputed each run, the regression gate is meaningless.
suggestions: Flip step 8 (OpenAPI spec) to be step 1.5 — define the API contract as a design artifact before any backend implementation. Use contract-first development with mock servers so frontend can develop in parallel against real schemas.

Replace 'redis_prefix_scan + semantic_rerank' with ZRANGEBYLEX on pre-built prefix keys or a dedicated trie (e.g., pyahocorasick, datrie). Pre-compute semantic scores offline and store ranked candidates per prefix key. Do not rerank at query time.

Add explicit HNSW parameter specification to step 4: ef_construct (128–256 for high recall), m (16–32), and ef at query time (64–128). Run a recall-vs-latency sweep on your corpus before setting these. Add acceptance criterion: 'HNSW params produce > 0.95 recall@10 vs brute-force at p95 < 100ms.'

Add a Qdrant payload index for every facet field (step 4) and benchmark both pre-filter and post-filter strategies against your expected filter selectivity distribution. Document the crossover point where post-filtering is preferred.

Replace the A/B framework in step 7 with a statistically rigorous design: define minimum detectable effect (e.g., 3% NDCG improvement), compute required sample size, implement sequential testing or fixed-horizon test with Bonferroni correction. Add acceptance criterion: 'experiment results include 95% confidence interval and required sample size.'

Add a write-coordinator service (or at minimum a saga pattern) to step 5 that ensures Qdrant upsert and BM25 index update are treated as a single logical operation with rollback or retry on partial failure. Log all index operations to the query_logs table for reconciliation.

Step 3 query expansion: specify the mechanism explicitly. LLM-based expansion adds 50–200ms and should be async/cached. Synonym dictionary (WordNet + domain-specific) is faster and more predictable. Decide which before implementation.
Add data retention and PII handling to step 16. Query logs (step 7) contain user search terms — define retention period, anonymization strategy, and GDPR deletion path before shipping.

Step 15 baseline versioning: store baseline run files in git-LFS or an artifact store (S3/GCS) keyed by a hash of the corpus + model version. CI fetches the appropriate baseline by key and stores new baselines only on explicit promotion.

Add a warm-up/seed strategy for autocomplete cold start. On first deploy, populate Redis prefix keys from the document corpus (titles, key phrases) so the autocomplete is useful before any query logs exist.
missing: Multi-language support: all-MiniLM-L6-v2 is trained primarily on English. No language detection, no multilingual model fallback (e.g., paraphrase-multilingual-MiniLM-L12-v2), no acceptance criterion about language coverage.

Qdrant authentication and API key management. Qdrant defaults to unauthenticated access. The security review (step 16) mentions JWT for the admin console but never addresses Qdrant's own API key or TLS configuration.

Backup and restore testing with acceptance criteria. Step 18 mentions a runbook but no step validates that a backup can actually be restored, or that Qdrant snapshots and BM25 index backups are taken at consistent points in time.

Scalability boundary documentation. The plan targets 100k documents but specifies no memory or storage estimates for Qdrant at that scale (384-dim float32 = ~150MB for 100k vectors plus HNSW graph overhead ~2–4x). No guidance on when horizontal sharding is needed.

Rate limiting on /autocomplete endpoint. Step 16 specifies limits for /search (100 req/min) and /index (10 req/min) but omits /autocomplete, which fires on every keypress and is the highest-volume endpoint by far.
HTTPS/TLS enforcement. No step configures TLS termination. A JWT-protected admin console served over HTTP is trivially compromised.
Dwell-time measurement implementation in the frontend. Step 7 lists dwell-time as a feedback signal but no frontend component or beacon API is planned in steps 10–11 to capture it.

Definition of 'bias evaluation' in step 3 and step 15. The term appears twice as an acceptance criterion with no methodology: what demographic or query-type bias is being measured, against what ground truth, with what pass/fail threshold?

Query deduplication and normalization for autocomplete index. As new queries arrive, misspellings and variations of the same intent will pollute the autocomplete index. No deduplication or normalization pipeline is specified.

Qdrant collection migration strategy. If the embedding model is upgraded (step 3 mentions fine-tuning), all 384-dim vectors become invalid and the entire collection must be reindexed. No zero-downtime reindexing strategy or dual-collection approach is defined.
security_risks: Qdrant runs unauthenticated by default. If the Qdrant port is reachable (even internally), any service can read, modify, or delete the entire vector collection. No authentication is specified in step 4 or step 16.

Redis sorted set for autocomplete has no authentication specified. Default Redis deployments have no password. Exposed Redis allows arbitrary key injection, autocomplete poisoning, and cache poisoning attacks.

Vector poisoning via the /index endpoint. Step 16 lists this as a review area but provides no mitigation. An attacker with index access (or a compromised upstream system) can inject adversarial documents with crafted embeddings that rank for sensitive queries. Input validation on document content does not prevent this.

JWT secret management is unaddressed. The plan specifies JWT for the admin console but does not define where the signing secret lives, how it is rotated, or how tokens are revoked. A hardcoded secret in an env file is a common failure mode.

Query logs store raw user search terms in PostgreSQL. If query_logs are not access-controlled separately from the main application DB credentials, any read access to the database exposes the full search history of all users.

Payload size limit (10MB on /index) is stated but chunking pipeline in step 2 processes PDFs and HTML. A compressed PDF can expand to 50–100x in text. The limit should apply post-decompression, not to the raw request body, or decompression bombs are possible.

No mention of output encoding for snippet highlighting in the frontend (step 10). Search result snippets render matched terms from user query input into the DOM. Without strict output encoding, a stored document containing HTML/JS can trigger XSS when highlighted.
final_review.summary: This is a well-structured, comprehensive plan with appropriate tech choices and measurable acceptance criteria across most steps. For an MVP semantic search engine it is above average in completeness. However, several architectural decisions will cause production failures rather than just technical debt: the inverted API design dependency (spec after implementation) guarantees schema drift; the autocomplete latency target is physically incompatible with semantic reranking at query time; the A/B framework lacks statistical validity and will produce misleading results; and index consistency between Qdrant and BM25 has no failure handling, making hybrid search correctness non-deterministic under load. The security posture has gaps that are easy to exploit (unauthenticated Qdrant, unauthenticated Redis, XSS in snippet rendering). The plan needs targeted fixes on these six issues before implementation begins — fixing them mid-build is significantly more expensive. Addressing these would bring the score to approximately 75–78 for MVP scope.

flaws: Step 8 (OpenAPI spec) depends on steps 5–7 (implementation) — this is design-after-code, not design-first. API contracts should be agreed before implementation to prevent schema drift and undocumented edge cases. The entire dependency chain is inverted here.

Step 6 autocomplete uses 'redis_prefix_scan + semantic_rerank' but targets < 20ms. Redis SCAN is O(N) and semantic reranking adds 30–80ms per call. These two constraints are physically incompatible unless reranking is fully pre-computed and cached — which is not specified.

Step 5 states facet filters are 'applied as pre-filter to vector search and BM25 simultaneously.' Qdrant pre-filtering degrades HNSW recall severely when selectivity is high (< 5% of corpus matches filter). Post-filtering or filtered HNSW (payload-indexed) trade-offs are not addressed — this will silently return poor results for selective filters.

Step 7 A/B experiment router has no statistical rigor: no minimum sample size, no significance threshold (p-value or Bayesian), no mechanism to determine when an experiment is conclusive. Traffic split percentage alone does not constitute a valid A/B framework. Shipping ranking changes based on inconclusive experiments is worse than no experiments.

Index consistency between Qdrant (vector) and BM25 (disk-serialized inverted index) is never addressed. A partial write failure leaves them diverged with no detection mechanism, no compensating transaction, and no reconciliation process. For hybrid search this is a correctness failure, not a latency issue.

Step 3 intent classifier (navigational/informational/transactional) has no training data source specified, no labeling process, no fallback behavior when confidence is low, and no acceptance criterion for the training set size. This is described as a deliverable but has no implementation path.

Step 2 claims '1000 docs/min on standard hardware' without specifying hardware. all-MiniLM-L6-v2 encoding on CPU achieves approximately 100–300 docs/min per core depending on chunk size. This target is probably unachievable without GPU or multi-worker parallelism, neither of which is planned.

Step 9 (UX design) only depends on step 1, but the relevance tuning console design should also depend on step 7. Technical constraints discovered in step 7 (e.g., hot-reload mechanics, boost rule schema) will force redesign of the console UI, discovered after wireframes are already approved.

Cursor-based pagination (step 10) is incompatible with dynamic relevance configs. If a relevance config changes between pages, cursor positions become invalid — documents can be duplicated or skipped across pages. No invalidation strategy is defined.

Step 15 regression test 'fails CI if NDCG@10 drops > 2%' requires a stable stored baseline. No mechanism for versioning, storing, or updating the baseline is specified. If the baseline is recomputed each run, the regression gate is meaningless.
suggestions: Flip step 8 (OpenAPI spec) to be step 1.5 — define the API contract as a design artifact before any backend implementation. Use contract-first development with mock servers so frontend can develop in parallel against real schemas.

Replace 'redis_prefix_scan + semantic_rerank' with ZRANGEBYLEX on pre-built prefix keys or a dedicated trie (e.g., pyahocorasick, datrie). Pre-compute semantic scores offline and store ranked candidates per prefix key. Do not rerank at query time.

Add explicit HNSW parameter specification to step 4: ef_construct (128–256 for high recall), m (16–32), and ef at query time (64–128). Run a recall-vs-latency sweep on your corpus before setting these. Add acceptance criterion: 'HNSW params produce > 0.95 recall@10 vs brute-force at p95 < 100ms.'

Add a Qdrant payload index for every facet field (step 4) and benchmark both pre-filter and post-filter strategies against your expected filter selectivity distribution. Document the crossover point where post-filtering is preferred.

Replace the A/B framework in step 7 with a statistically rigorous design: define minimum detectable effect (e.g., 3% NDCG improvement), compute required sample size, implement sequential testing or fixed-horizon test with Bonferroni correction. Add acceptance criterion: 'experiment results include 95% confidence interval and required sample size.'

Add a write-coordinator service (or at minimum a saga pattern) to step 5 that ensures Qdrant upsert and BM25 index update are treated as a single logical operation with rollback or retry on partial failure. Log all index operations to the query_logs table for reconciliation.

Step 3 query expansion: specify the mechanism explicitly. LLM-based expansion adds 50–200ms and should be async/cached. Synonym dictionary (WordNet + domain-specific) is faster and more predictable. Decide which before implementation.
Add data retention and PII handling to step 16. Query logs (step 7) contain user search terms — define retention period, anonymization strategy, and GDPR deletion path before shipping.

Step 15 baseline versioning: store baseline run files in git-LFS or an artifact store (S3/GCS) keyed by a hash of the corpus + model version. CI fetches the appropriate baseline by key and stores new baselines only on explicit promotion.

Add a warm-up/seed strategy for autocomplete cold start. On first deploy, populate Redis prefix keys from the document corpus (titles, key phrases) so the autocomplete is useful before any query logs exist.
missing: Multi-language support: all-MiniLM-L6-v2 is trained primarily on English. No language detection, no multilingual model fallback (e.g., paraphrase-multilingual-MiniLM-L12-v2), no acceptance criterion about language coverage.

Qdrant authentication and API key management. Qdrant defaults to unauthenticated access. The security review (step 16) mentions JWT for the admin console but never addresses Qdrant's own API key or TLS configuration.

Backup and restore testing with acceptance criteria. Step 18 mentions a runbook but no step validates that a backup can actually be restored, or that Qdrant snapshots and BM25 index backups are taken at consistent points in time.

Scalability boundary documentation. The plan targets 100k documents but specifies no memory or storage estimates for Qdrant at that scale (384-dim float32 = ~150MB for 100k vectors plus HNSW graph overhead ~2–4x). No guidance on when horizontal sharding is needed.

Rate limiting on /autocomplete endpoint. Step 16 specifies limits for /search (100 req/min) and /index (10 req/min) but omits /autocomplete, which fires on every keypress and is the highest-volume endpoint by far.
HTTPS/TLS enforcement. No step configures TLS termination. A JWT-protected admin console served over HTTP is trivially compromised.
Dwell-time measurement implementation in the frontend. Step 7 lists dwell-time as a feedback signal but no frontend component or beacon API is planned in steps 10–11 to capture it.

Definition of 'bias evaluation' in step 3 and step 15. The term appears twice as an acceptance criterion with no methodology: what demographic or query-type bias is being measured, against what ground truth, with what pass/fail threshold?

Query deduplication and normalization for autocomplete index. As new queries arrive, misspellings and variations of the same intent will pollute the autocomplete index. No deduplication or normalization pipeline is specified.

Qdrant collection migration strategy. If the embedding model is upgraded (step 3 mentions fine-tuning), all 384-dim vectors become invalid and the entire collection must be reindexed. No zero-downtime reindexing strategy or dual-collection approach is defined.
security_risks: Qdrant runs unauthenticated by default. If the Qdrant port is reachable (even internally), any service can read, modify, or delete the entire vector collection. No authentication is specified in step 4 or step 16.

Redis sorted set for autocomplete has no authentication specified. Default Redis deployments have no password. Exposed Redis allows arbitrary key injection, autocomplete poisoning, and cache poisoning attacks.

Vector poisoning via the /index endpoint. Step 16 lists this as a review area but provides no mitigation. An attacker with index access (or a compromised upstream system) can inject adversarial documents with crafted embeddings that rank for sensitive queries. Input validation on document content does not prevent this.

JWT secret management is unaddressed. The plan specifies JWT for the admin console but does not define where the signing secret lives, how it is rotated, or how tokens are revoked. A hardcoded secret in an env file is a common failure mode.

Query logs store raw user search terms in PostgreSQL. If query_logs are not access-controlled separately from the main application DB credentials, any read access to the database exposes the full search history of all users.

Payload size limit (10MB on /index) is stated but chunking pipeline in step 2 processes PDFs and HTML. A compressed PDF can expand to 50–100x in text. The limit should apply post-decompression, not to the raw request body, or decompression bombs are possible.

No mention of output encoding for snippet highlighting in the frontend (step 10). Search result snippets render matched terms from user query input into the DOM. Without strict output encoding, a stored document containing HTML/JS can trigger XSS when highlighted.
history.summary: This is a well-structured, comprehensive plan with appropriate tech choices and measurable acceptance criteria across most steps. For an MVP semantic search engine it is above average in completeness. However, several architectural decisions will cause production failures rather than just technical debt: the inverted API design dependency (spec after implementation) guarantees schema drift; the autocomplete latency target is physically incompatible with semantic reranking at query time; the A/B framework lacks statistical validity and will produce misleading results; and index consistency between Qdrant and BM25 has no failure handling, making hybrid search correctness non-deterministic under load. The security posture has gaps that are easy to exploit (unauthenticated Qdrant, unauthenticated Redis, XSS in snippet rendering). The plan needs targeted fixes on these six issues before implementation begins — fixing them mid-build is significantly more expensive. Addressing these would bring the score to approximately 75–78 for MVP scope.
